steps.prompt: Which is the main finite verb?

hints.clamans: 'Clamans' is a present active participle meaning 'crying out'
hints.voce: 'Voce' is a noun in the ablative meaning 'with a voice'
hints.Iesus: 'Iesus' is a proper noun — the subject

hints.to go: 'To go' is 'ire' — 'ait' is a defective verb meaning 'he says/said'
hints.to hear: 'To hear' is 'audire' — 'ait' comes from the defective verb 'aio'
hints.to think: 'To think' is 'cogitare' — 'ait' is from the defective 'aio' (to say)

steps.target: perfect indicative (or present — defective verb, context determines)
choices: perfect indicative (or present — defective verb, context determines)
steps.reveal: ait = he said (defective verb — same form for present and perfect)

hints.imperfect indicative: Imperfect of 'aio' would be 'aiebat' — 'ait' is present/perfect
hints.present subjunctive: 'Aio' lacks a full subjunctive paradigm — 'ait' is indicative
hints.future indicative: 'Aio' lacks a future tense — 'ait' is present/perfect indicative

hints.ago, agere, egi, actum: These are the parts of 'ago' (to do/drive)
hints.audio, audire, audivi, auditum: These are the parts of 'audio' (to hear)
hints.dico, dicere, dixi, dictum: These are the parts of 'dico' (to say) — 'aio' is a separate defective verb

steps.reveal: clamans = crying out (pres. act. part., circumstantial)

hints.perfect passive participle: Perfect passive participle would be 'clamatus' — 'clamans' has -ans (present)
hints.gerundive: Gerundive would be 'clamandus' — 'clamans' is a present participle
hints.finite verb: 'Clamans' is a participle, not a finite verb

hints.claudo, claudere, clausi, clausum: These are the parts of 'claudo' (to close/shut)
hints.clango, clangere, —, —: These are the parts of 'clango' (to clang/resound)
hints.celo, celare, celavi, celatum: These are the parts of 'celo' (to hide)

steps.reveal: voce magna = with a loud voice (abl. of manner)

hints.nominative: Nominative would be 'vox' — 'voce' with -e is ablative

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'vocis' ends in -is
hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'vocis' ends in -is
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -us — 'vocis' ends in -is

steps.prompt: What type of ablative is 'voce magna'?
steps.reveal: voce magna = with a great voice / in a loud voice (manner without 'cum' because of the adjective)

hints.ablative of means: Ablative of means names an instrument — 'voce' describes the manner of speaking
hints.ablative absolute: Ablative absolute needs a noun + participle forming an independent clause — this modifies the main verb
hints.ablative of accompaniment: Ablative of accompaniment uses 'cum' for persons — this describes the manner of the cry

translation: And crying out with a loud voice, Jesus said
